enriched_points.expanded_content: The Enhanced Permeability and Retention (EPR) effect facilitates nanoparticles' accumulation in tumor tissues due to leaky vasculature and impaired lymphatic drainage.
Nanoparticles exploit the EPR effect, allowing higher drug concentration within tumors compared to normal tissues, enhancing therapeutic efficacy.
Passive targeting reduces systemic toxicity, improves drug bioavailability, and simplifies formulation processes, making it a favorable strategy for cancer therapy.
EPR heterogeneity across tumors and variable nanoparticle sizes can hinder effective targeting, necessitating optimization in drug delivery systems.

enriched_points.point_title: Examples of passive targeting in use
enriched_points.expanded_content: Doxil and Abraxane are notable examples, utilizing the EPR effect to deliver chemotherapeutic agents effectively to tumors.
